result:
Rain has a way of changing the scale of the world. On a dry day, everything feels available: streets stretch outward, errands multiply, and people move as if there is always somewhere more important to be. But when rain begins, especially the steady kind that does not announce itself with drama, the world seems to draw inward. Windows become borders. Lamps become warmer. The distance between one thought and the next gets shorter.

I like the moment just before a storm settles in, when the air feels charged and leaves turn their pale undersides toward the sky. It is a small rehearsal for surrender. Birds go quiet. Sidewalks empty. Even buildings seem to pause, as if remembering that for all their angles and steel, they still stand under weather.

Then the rain arrives and starts editing the city. Harsh outlines soften. Reflections appear in broken pavement. Familiar places become briefly unfamiliar, and therefore interesting again. A grocery store under rain looks almost cinematic. A bus stop becomes a tiny fellowship of strangers, each person pretending not to notice the others while sharing the same thin shelter.

What I enjoy most is how rain gives permission: permission to slow down, to postpone, to listen. It turns productivity into a less convincing religion. It asks for tea, for books, for the acceptance that not every hour must be optimized. The earth drinks without apology. Trees shine. Gutters chatter. And somewhere in all that gray, the day becomes more itself, not less.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
